column4: The yield on 2-year government bonds, closely tracking the central bank policy rate. Ranges from 0% to 20%. Current developed-economy norms are 2-5%. For eurozone members this is heavily influenced by ECB policy. A value of 0.15 on the scale represents approximately 3%. Higher rates signal tighter monetary conditions slowing growth but containing inflation.

The benchmark 10-year government bond yield, the single most important interest rate in the economy. Ranges from 0% to 20%. Spain 10Y yield is typically 3-4%, Italy 4-5%, Germany 2-3%, and crisis-era Greece hit 35%. This rate directly sets mortgage costs, corporate borrowing rates, and the governments cost of rolling over debt.

The 30-year government bond yield reflecting the markets long-run outlook. Ranges from 0% to 20%. The 30Y yield captures the term premium, extra compensation for locking money up for three decades. It particularly affects infrastructure investment and pension fund returns.

The spread between 30-year and 2-year bond yields. At 0.5 on the bar the curve is flat. Above 0.5 means a normal upward-sloping curve. Below 0.5 means inversion, historically the most reliable recession predictor. A normal healthy spread is 100-200 basis points.

The inflation-adjusted interest rate. At 0.5 the real rate is approximately 0%. Above 0.5 means positive real rates attracting foreign capital. Below 0.5 means negative real rates encouraging risk-taking but potentially fueling bubbles.

The share of government revenue consumed by interest payments. At 0.25+ on the scale the government enters doom-loop territory where higher interest costs force more borrowing which raises rates further.

The extra yield investors demand for credit risk. At 0.1 risk is low (AAA-rated). At 0.3 moderate stress (BBB). At 0.5+ crisis levels approaching junk territory. This feeds directly into all bond yields creating the doom loop mechanism.

Total money circulating in the economy. At 0.5 money supply is at equilibrium. QE expands it. Excessive expansion drives inflation and currency weakness. Contraction strengthens the currency but can trigger credit crunches.

GDP measured in current prices without inflation adjustment. Unlike real GDP nominal GDP rises with both genuine economic growth and rising prices. It is the figure most relevant for debt-to-GDP calculations since government debt is denominated in nominal terms.

Government revenue minus spending. At 0.5 on scale the budget is balanced. Below 0.5 is deficit and above is surplus. Persistent deficits accumulate debt and eventually increase sovereign risk. Fiscal consolidation is politically painful but essential when debt service costs begin spiraling.

Purchasing-power-parity adjusted GDP per person, the best measure of actual living standards. This adjusts for the fact that the same currency buys different amounts in different countries. Rising PPP per capita attracts immigration and reduces emigration.

The extra yield investors demand for holding longer-duration bonds versus rolling over short-term ones. Historically 50-150 basis points. Rising term premiums push up long-end yields and tighten financial conditions even without central bank action.
